choices.text: Default `kill PID` sends SIGTERM — a polite request that the process can catch and respond to by saving state, closing files, releasing locks, finishing in-flight work, and exiting cleanly. Most well-written processes do exactly this. `kill -9` sends SIGKILL, which can't be caught — the kernel terminates the process immediately, no cleanup. The cost: orphaned lock files, half-written data, corrupt databases, dependent processes left hanging. Escalation rule: SIGTERM → wait 5-10 seconds → if still running, SIGKILL. Production services routinely handle SIGTERM gracefully; SIGKILL is the firefighter's axe, useful but destructive. Reserve it for genuinely stuck processes that ignore SIGTERM.

questions.explanation: SIGTERM is catchable — allows cleanup. SIGKILL is uncatchable and leaves messes.

questions.prompt: Your shell script creates a temp file in /tmp/work and gets Ctrl-C'd mid-run. File is left behind. Fix?

choices.text: Add a `trap` that cleans up on signal: `trap 'rm -f /tmp/work; exit 1' SIGINT SIGTERM EXIT`. Bash's trap registers a handler for signals; placed near the top of the script, it ensures cleanup runs regardless of how the script ends — normal exit, Ctrl-C interrupt, SIGTERM from killing, or even an unhandled error. The `EXIT` pseudo-signal fires for ANY exit path; combining it with SIGINT/SIGTERM covers both 'crashed/killed' and 'finished normally.' Cleanup actions go in the trap; the script's main body focuses on logic. Standard pattern for any script that creates state outside its own variables: lock files, temp dirs, downloaded artifacts, half-built outputs. Without trap, debris accumulates over time and eventually breaks something.

questions.explanation: trap cleanup_cmd SIGINT SIGTERM EXIT — runs cleanup regardless of how script exits.
